nativeRange.habitat: Shallow wave-exposed reef flats, reef crests, and intertidal zones; occasionally found in marginal habitats

decorPreferences: open placement with spacing from neighbors

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via symbiotic zooxanthellae. Supplemental feeding with zooplankton, phytoplankton, and small meaty particulates has been reported to benefit growth and polyp extension. Supplementation with calcium, strontium, iodine, and trace elements is generally advised; notably, iron supplementation should be avoided as the skeleton naturally sequesters iron salts for its characteristic blue coloration.

avoidWith: aggressive LPS corals with sweeper tentacles placed directly adjacent

summary: Blue Ridge Coral (Heliopora coerulea) is the only known octocoral to build a true calcified skeleton, tinted a distinctive blue by iron salts absorbed from seawater. Listed on CITES Appendix II and IUCN Vulnerable, it demands high flow, strong light, and stable reef water chemistry.
careNotes:
Blue Ridge Coral is a remarkable biological outlier: it is the only surviving member of the order Helioporacea and the only octocoral — a group of eight-tentacled corals that includes soft corals such as Xenia and gorgonians — to build a true massive calcified skeleton. Despite its rigid skeleton, which resembles that of an SPS (stony) coral, Blue Ridge Coral is classified as a soft coral based on its octocoral anatomy: each polyp bears eight pinnate (feathered) tentacles, a defining characteristic of the subclass Octocorallia. The family Helioporidae contains only this single species, Heliopora coerulea.

The coral's distinctive sky-blue skeleton results from iron salts that the colony absorbs from surrounding seawater and locks into its fibrocrystalline aragonite skeleton. This same blue pigmentation has allowed scientists to identify the species in the fossil record back to the Cretaceous period, earning it the designation of a living fossil. DO NOT add supplemental iron to an aquarium housing this coral — Blue Ridge Coral manages its own iron uptake naturally, and excess iron in the water column can harm the broader reef ecosystem.

Key care considerations:
• Lighting: 150–300 PAR is generally advised. New frags should be introduced at the lower end of this range and acclimated upward gradually.
• Flow: High, chaotic water movement is generally advised, reflecting the species' natural habitat on wave-exposed reef crests. Direct laminar flow aimed at the polyps should be avoided.
• Water chemistry: Stable calcium (380–430 ppm), alkalinity (8–12 dKH), and magnesium (1250–1400 ppm) are required. Sudden swings in any of these parameters should be avoided. This species is generally considered somewhat more tolerant of minor fluctuations than typical SPS corals, owing to its soft-coral physiology.
• Growth forms: Blue Ridge Coral exhibits variable morphology — branching, encrusting, plating, and tubular forms are all known. In the aquarium it tends to encrust over rockwork and glass. Wild colonies can reach 70–100 cm in height or diameter, while aquarium frags typically grow slowly to 20–30 cm over several years.
• CITES Appendix II: International trade in this species is regulated. Aquacultured frags are the recommended source for the hobby and are available from specialty coral vendors. Wild-collected specimens require CITES export documentation.
• Placement: Middle to upper rockwork placement is generally advised to maximize light exposure.
• Blue Ridge Coral has been reported to lack sweeper tentacles, making it less aggressive toward neighboring corals than many LPS species. Standard spacing from neighbors is still advised to allow room for natural encrusting growth.
• Conservation: Listed as Vulnerable on the IUCN Red List. Wild populations are threatened by bleaching events, ocean acidification, habitat destruction, and collection pressure.
breedingNotes: Blue Ridge Coral can be fragged by cutting or breaking branches; the exposed skeleton tends to heal and encrust over time. In the wild, sexual reproduction occurs via broadcast spawning, but deliberate captive sexual breeding is not practiced in the hobby. Aquacultured frags are commercially available from specialty coral vendors and are preferred over wild-collected specimens given the species' CITES Appendix II status.

primary.notes: Primary husbandry source. Provides temperature 72–78°F, salinity 1.023–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, alkalinity 8–12 dKH, lighting moderate to high, flow medium to strong, care level moderate.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Heliopora coerulea

Not used for husbandry parameters.

additional.name: AquariumDomain – Blue Ridge Coral
additional.notes: Confirmed temperature 72–79°F (22–26°C), salinity 1.023–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, alkalinity 8–12 dKH, flow moderate to strong, placement middle to high, care level easy. Noted lack of sweeper tentacles.

additional.notes: Cross-checked lighting (50–150 PAR reported, though higher recommended), temperature 24–28°C, flow moderate, care level moderate to easy. PAR range lower bound informed decision to note acclimation range in careNotes.
